apps.name: OpenMasjid Display
apps.tagline: Run prayer timetables, cameras and HDMI on every screen in your masjid — over your network
apps.author: hasan-ismail
apps.description:
**OpenMasjid Display** turns one small computer into the control room for every
screen in your masjid. Each TV gets its own network video link (RTSP) that you
point a cheap "RTSP decoder" box at once — then you decide, from your phone or a
computer, what each screen shows:

- **Prayer timetables** — beautiful, full-screen prayer clocks calculated on the
  device (no internet needed). Make as many as you like, each with its own colours
  to match the room it hangs in. Shows a large live clock, the Hijri and Gregorian
  dates, every prayer's Adhan **and** Iqamah time, Jumu'ah, and a gentle countdown
  to the next prayer with the current prayer highlighted.
- **Cameras** — bring in any security/IP camera or an imam camera and put it on a
  screen with one tap (great for overflow rooms and the women's section).
- **HDMI sources** — plug a laptop or a recording into an HDMI-to-network encoder
  and send it to the screens you choose.

Set it once and forget it: a weekly **schedule** can switch a screen to a camera
for Jumu'ah, then back to the timetable afterwards — automatically. Volunteers can
always take over instantly from the simple mobile page.

It's designed to be light enough for a Raspberry Pi, a mini-PC, or a Proxmox
container, and the whole control panel matches the calm OpenMasjidOS look in both
light and dark.

**Nothing to fill in here — just install.** When it's running, click **Open** —
it signs you in with your OpenMasjidOS login and matches your dashboard's light/dark
look and wallpaper. Then set everything up inside: your masjid details, your screens,
cameras and schedules. Each screen shows you the exact link to paste into its decoder —
no network address to look up.
apps.compose:
# OpenMasjid Display — one container that runs everything:
#   • the control panel (web UI + API),
#   • the timetable video renderer, and
#   • the RTSP server (MediaMTX) every screen's decoder connects to.
# Bundling them keeps it simple: one image to install, one to update. It stays
# least-privilege: no privileged mode, host networking, devices or sockets.
#
# Nothing to configure at install time: install it, open the control panel,
# create your password, and set everything up inside the app (saved to /data).
# Screens find this server automatically from the address you open the panel with.
services:
  app:
    # Tag for humans + digest for integrity (the @sha256 is what actually pins). A
    # moved tag can't repoint this to a different image. Bump both each release.
    image: ghcr.io/hasan-ismail/openmasjiddisplay:0.20.7@sha256:13fa37f4089edb1d0405a185109c3b5a12ca557d8c90efc0134794d031d191b3
    restart: unless-stopped
    environment:
      PORT: "8080"
      VOLUNTEER_PORT: "8081"
      VOLUNTEER_PUBLIC_PORT: "7861"
      DATA_DIR: /data
      # OpenMasjidOS Fabric: the platform writes these into the app's .env and runs
      # compose with --env-file, which only feeds ${VAR} SUBSTITUTION — so we must
      # reference them here for them to actually reach the container (appearance/SSO +
      # notifications). Empty defaults keep a standalone `docker compose up` quiet.
      OPENMASJID_BASE_URL: ${OPENMASJID_BASE_URL:-}
      OPENMASJID_APP_ID: ${OPENMASJID_APP_ID:-}
      OPENMASJID_APP_SECRET: ${OPENMASJID_APP_SECRET:-}
    ports:
      # host:container. OpenMasjidOS offers "Open" on the LOWEST published port,
      # so the control panel (7860) must stay below the RTSP port (8554). The
      # platform picks a different free host port automatically if one is taken.
      - "7860:8080"
      # The simple mobile volunteer page (off by default; turn it on in Settings).
      - "7861:8081"
      # RTSP video — each screen's decoder connects here (TCP).
      - "8554:8554"
    volumes:
      - data:/data

volumes:
  data:

apps.name: OpenMasjid Donations
apps.tagline: Take card donations on your masjid's network with Stripe
apps.author: hasan-ismail
apps.description:
**OpenMasjid Donations** gives your masjid a beautiful, self-hosted donation
website powered by **Stripe**. Put it on a screen by the door with a QR code, or
open it on any phone — a supporter picks a cause, chooses a preset or their own
amount (one-time or monthly), and pays securely by card.

- **Appeals you control** — create as many as you like (General Fund, Zakat,
  Building Fund, Ramadan Appeal…), each with its own wording, images, preset
  amounts and an optional goal.
- **Preset *and* custom amounts** — clear suggested buttons plus a "choose your
  own" field, one-time or monthly.
- **Card payments, on your own page** — powered by Stripe's secure card field.
  Your masjid's card details and keys stay on your device; supporters' card
  numbers go straight to Stripe and never touch this app.
- **A simple admin panel** — manage appeals, see your donations and totals,
  export a spreadsheet, and set your theme to match the rest of OpenMasjidOS.

**Nothing to fill in here — just install.** When it's running, click **Open** —
it signs you in with your OpenMasjidOS login and matches your dashboard's
light/dark look and wallpaper. Then add your Stripe keys and your first appeal
inside the app. It runs happily on a Raspberry Pi or mini-PC, on your masjid's
own network.
apps.compose:
# OpenMasjid Donations — one container that runs everything: the donation site,
# the admin panel, the API, and the SQLite data store. One image to install, one
# to update. It stays least-privilege: no privileged mode, no host networking, no
# devices, no Docker socket.
#
# Nothing to configure at install time: install it, open it, and it takes you
# straight to setup — sign in, then add your Stripe keys and your first appeal
# inside the app (saved to /data).
services:
  app:
    # Tag for humans + @sha256 digest for integrity (the digest is the real pin — a
    # moved tag can't repoint it to a different image). Bump both on every release.
    image: ghcr.io/hasan-ismail/openmasjiddonations:0.4.1@sha256:8d49640ee758acf04a814592ab1081571a26b809b2e974ff047898061a04effd
    restart: unless-stopped
    environment:
      PORT: "8080"
      DATA_DIR: /data
      # OpenMasjidOS Fabric: the platform writes these into the app's .env and runs
      # compose with --env-file, which only feeds ${VAR} SUBSTITUTION — so they must
      # be referenced here to actually reach the container (appearance/SSO +
      # notifications). Empty defaults keep a standalone `docker compose up` quiet.
      OPENMASJID_BASE_URL: ${OPENMASJID_BASE_URL:-}
      OPENMASJID_APP_ID: ${OPENMASJID_APP_ID:-}
      OPENMASJID_APP_SECRET: ${OPENMASJID_APP_SECRET:-}
      # Optional install settings (also settable in the admin panel). Delivered the
      # same way — ${KEY} substitution from the platform-written .env. The secret
      # key is read server-side only and never sent to the browser.
      STRIPE_PUBLISHABLE_KEY: ${STRIPE_PUBLISHABLE_KEY:-}
      STRIPE_SECRET_KEY: ${STRIPE_SECRET_KEY:-}
      STRIPE_WEBHOOK_SECRET: ${STRIPE_WEBHOOK_SECRET:-}
      CURRENCY: ${CURRENCY:-}
    ports:
      # host:container. OpenMasjidOS offers "Open" on the lowest published port and
      # picks a different free host port automatically if 7870 is already taken.
      - "7870:8080"
    volumes:
      - data:/data
    # Least-privilege hardening that is safe alongside the named data volume. (Full
    # non-root + read-only rootfs needs an entrypoint to chown the volume and is
    # planned once validated in CI; the container otherwise mirrors Display.)
    cap_drop:
      - ALL
    security_opt:
      - no-new-privileges:true
    tmpfs:
      - /tmp

volumes:
  data:

apps.name: OpenMasjid Kiosk
apps.tagline: An Android-tablet donation kiosk — take in-person card donations with a Stripe M2 card reader.

apps.name: OpenMasjid Student Manager
apps.tagline: Run your madrasah — students, classes, attendance and progress.
